THE IRISH BOOK

A CORK BIBLIOGRAPHICAL PUZZLE.

In the list of books believed to be printed in Cork in the 17th
Century there is an item entitled, ‘‘ Scripture Evidences for Baptising
the Children of Covenanters,” consisting of twa sermons preached in
Cork by the Rev. Edward Worth; one in April and the other in June,
1653. The volume is a substantial quarto and I have only handled
two copies, i.e., one in the “ Worth Library,” connected' with Madam
Steeven’s Hospital, Dublin, and the other, which I have recently seen,
amongst the “ Cashel Diocesan ’’ Library, books lent to Marsh's
Library. Except for the date in the title there is no date anywhere in
the volume and the imprint runs thus: “ Printed for T. Taylor,
Widow, and are to be sold at her shop in Cork.” I have carefully
examined all the ornaments, initial letters, type, etc., and find the
examination of the work reveals the following curious facts:—

(a) The crnament at the top and bottom of the title page (and in
other places in the volume, i.e., at p. 6), occurs In a Proclamation by
the Coniederate Catholic Council printed by Thomas Bourke in
Waterford in 1644, and in a pamphlet printed there in 1652 by Peter
de Pienne for the Cromwellian Party.

(b) The * factotum >’ which occurs twice in 1t, at the second leal
(Advice “ To the Reader ) and at p. 1, occurs in two of the Cork-printed
Tracts of 1648.

(c) Turning again to the second leaf in the volume (“To the
Reader *’), we find at the top an ornament which occurs also in Water-
ford printing of 1644, and so does the ornament &t the top of the verso
at leaf 3 (“ The Printer to the Reader ™), as well as the; ornament 2
the top of p. 146. This latter ornament indeed occurs throughout this
volume though in varied form or position, or combined with another

It is clear that this volume must have been printed after 16353,
or, at least, very late in that year. How then did it come to pass
that so much of the type, ornaments, eftc., used by Bourke in Water-
ford some ten years earlier, and by de Pienne a year or two prior,
were used in printing this volume? I should like to get some explana-
tion of this and perhaps some of our readers who have local know-
ledge can give it. Apparently either thework was printed in Water-
ford, and not in Cork, by whoever had possession in 1653 or 1654 of
Bourke’s press, or that press had been brought to Cork from Water-
ford and the work then printed there. The Cromwellians gained pos-
session of Waterford in 1650-1 and used the press, in their possession
tthere, for printing matter of their own in 1651 and 1652.

I may mention! that the printing in this volume is not a bit like
the printing of William Smith who was a printer in Cork at least
in 1656 and during many later years.

The late Henry Bradshaw argued that there was printed in Water-
ford in 1654 “ Parthenissa,” a Romance, by Lord Orrery, though no
copy 18 known. There is a Tract in the British Museum dated 1654
“/A Copy of a Letter from an Officer, etc., to the Protector,” ete.,
dated at Waterford, but without imprint, which may have been printed
there. | -

I might by the way also mention as curious and worth noting, that
when the Confederate Catholics moved from Waterford to Kilkenny
and carried on printing there, the ornaments used in their * Kil-
kenny ”’ press were quite different from those used in their “ Water-
ford” press. Perhaps therefore they lost the press in Waterford or it

got into other hands and was used there or brought to Cork before -

1664, and that thev were obliged to get another press for printing their
Publications in Kilkenny. If any reader can throw any light on this
point T shall be very glad. E. R. Mc. C. DIX.

FORTHCOMING WORKS.

As an outcome of the Ferguson Centenary, a complete edition of
his works will be published, the poetry to be edited by Mr. Graves,
and the prose works by Mr. Bigger.

Messrs. Dent will publish shortly, a volume entitled “ Ireland from
the Unionq to Catholic Emancipation,” by D. A. Chart, who has had
access to a large number of unpublished documents throwing new
light on the social and economic¢al conditions of Ireland during the
early part of the 19th century.

The 1ate John Boyle O’Reilly, soldier, IFFenian, convict, and editor
of the ‘“ Boston Pilot,” was a poet of no mean power, and it 1s gratify-
ing to learn that we are shortly to have a volume of selections from
his published works. The book will be edited by his daughter, who has
inherited much of her father’s poetic ability, and will comprise those
poems most typical of his art as a poet.

I stated last month that the ‘“Life of Mr. John Redmond was
‘off.”’”> Instead, we are to have a volume of his ‘“ Speeches on Home
Rule,”’ 1883-1909, from the firm of Fisher Unwin. The book will be
edited by Mr. Barry O’Brien, who contributes an introduction. It will
be remembered that a volume of speeches delivered by the Irish leader
in the autumn of 1907, with a preface and notes by Mr. Swift MacNeill,
was published by Sealey, Bryers in January, 1908 (8vo., pp. 100.)

I’ve heard the lark’s cry thrill the sky o’er the meadows of Lusk,
And the first joyous gush of the thrush from Adare’s April wood.
At thy lone music’s spell, Philomel, magic-stricken I’ve stood,
When in Espan afar star on star trembled out of the dusk,

- While Dunkerron,s blue dove murmured love neath her nest I have

- sighed, -

And by mazy Culdaff with a laugh mocked the cuckoo’s refrain;
Derrycairn’s dusky bird I have heard piping joy hard by pain,

~ And the swan’s last lament sobbing sent over Moyle’s mystic tide -
Yet as brlght shadows pass from the glass of the darkening lake,

- As the rose’s rapt ﬁlgh must die when the zephyr ia chilled,

ln oblwion grey sleeps each lay that those birds ever trilled—

CURRENT CATALOGUES.

We would direct special attention to the catalogue just issued by
Messrs. Hanna and Neale, of 18, Nassau Street, Dublin. It contains
about 1,200 items of Irish desiderata, and from the samples we have
selected, we can bear our personal testimony to their fine condition
and moderate price. It is the finest collection we have seen for many
8 day. We would point out one slip probably due to the printer, a
copy of the first (1861) edition of Lecky’s ““ Leaders,” is priced at four
guineas, and said to be one of the thirty sold. We have Lecky’s own
testimony that only three were sold, as mentioned in our pages some
months ago, a fact which led the “ Daily News,” In quoting our re-
marks, to declare that that was in their opinion, the smallest number
of copies sold of any book on record.
